content:
# Acts Chapter 22 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Acts 22
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 253-254

---

## Summary Statistics
- **Total verses:** 30
- **Verses with disagreements:** 29
- **Total individual disagreements logged:** 95
- **Categories:** Word choice, structural/syntactic, interpretive expansion, theological/textual

---

## Full Disagreement Table

| Verse | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | "Men, brothers and fathers" | "Brothers and fathers" | "Brothers and fathers" | "Brothers and fathers" | LOGOS/PNEUMA omit ANDRES as redundant vocative address; EMET "Men, brothers" mirrors Greek ANDRES ADELPHOI but is awkward in English |
| 1 | "hear my defense [which is] now to you" | "hear my defense which I now make to you" | "hear me out -- let me give you my defense" | "hear my defense which I now make to you" | LOGOS supplies "I make" naturally for implied verb; PNEUMA restructures into two clauses with dash; EMET brackets supplement |
| 2 | "having-heard that in-the Hebrew dialect he-was-addressing them" | "when they heard that he was addressing them in the Hebrew language" | "When they heard him speaking in the Hebrew language" | "when they heard that he was addressing them in the Hebrew language" | LOGOS temporal clause natural; EMET "dialect" for DIALEKTO is valid but "language" is more natural English; PNEUMA "speaking" for PROSEPHONEI is weaker than "addressing" |
| 2 | "they-showed more quietness" | "they became even more quiet" | "they grew even quieter" | "they became even more quiet" | LOGOS renders PARECHON HESUCHIAN idiomatically; PNEUMA "grew quieter" is acceptable; EMET "showed more quietness" is stilted |
| 2 | "And he-says" | "And he said" | "Then he said" | "And he said" | EMET preserves historic present PHESIN; LOGOS/SOPHIA normalize to past tense for English consistency; PNEUMA adds "Then" not in text |
| 3 | "I am a man, a Jew" | "I am a Jewish man" | "I am a Jew" | "I am a Jewish man" | LOGOS combines ANER IOUDAIOS into "Jewish man"; EMET preserves apposition "a man, a Jew"; PNEUMA drops ANER entirely |
| 3 | "having-been-born in Tarsus of-Cilicia" | "born in Tarsus of Cilicia" | "born in Tarsus of Cilicia" | "born in Tarsus of Cilicia" | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree; EMET hyphenated participle |
| 3 | "having-been-brought-up in this city" | "brought up in this city" | "raised right here in this city" | "brought up in this city" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "right here" adds emphasis not in text; EMET hyphenated participle |
| 3 | "at the feet of Gamaliel, having-been-instructed according-to [the] exactness of-the ancestral law" | "educated at the feet of Gamaliel according to the strictness of the ancestral law" | "I sat at Gamaliel's feet and was trained with strict precision in our ancestral Law" | "educated at the feet of Gamaliel according to the strictness of the ancestral law" | LOGOS "educated" for PEPAIDEUEMENOS captures full pedagogical scope; PNEUMA "sat at...feet" restructures, "trained with strict precision" is interpretive expansion, possessive "our" and capitalized "Law" added; EMET "having-been-instructed" is narrower than PAIDEUO; LOGOS "strictness" for AKRIBEIAN best |
| 3 | "being a zealot of-God just-as all you are today" | "being zealous for God just as all of you are today" | "I burned with zeal for God -- exactly the way every one of you does today" | "being zealous for God just as all of you are today" | LOGOS natural rendering of ZELOTES HUPARCHON TOU THEOU; PNEUMA "burned with zeal" is vivid but interpretive expansion; EMET "a zealot of-God" is literal but could be confused with the Zealot party |
| 4 | "Who persecuted this Way unto death" | "I persecuted this Way unto death" | "I hunted down the followers of this Way to the death" | "I persecuted this Way unto death" | EMET relative pronoun "Who" mirrors Greek HOS but is awkward; LOGOS/SOPHIA use "I" for clarity; PNEUMA "hunted down" is interpretive for EDIOKSA and adds "followers of" not in text |
| 4 | "binding and delivering-over into prisons both men and women" | "binding and delivering into prisons both men and women" | "I had men and women alike bound in chains and thrown into prison" | "binding and delivering into prisons both men and women" | EMET/LOGOS agree on participial structure; PNEUMA adds "in chains" and "thrown into" (dramatic expansion) |
| 5 | "the high-priest bears-witness to-me" | "the high priest bears me witness" | "The high priest himself can confirm this" | "the high priest bears me witness" | LOGOS natural dative placement; PNEUMA "himself can confirm this" is interpretive; EMET "bears-witness to-me" is literal |
| 5 | "all the council-of-elders" | "all the council of elders" | "the whole council of elders" | "all the council of elders" | EMET/LOGOS "all" for PAN; PNEUMA "whole" is acceptable synonym |
| 5 | "from whom also having-received letters to the brothers" | "from whom I also received letters to the brothers" | "I even carried letters from them to our brothers in Damascus" | "from whom I also received letters to the brothers" | LOGOS preserves "received" (DEXAMENOS); PNEUMA "carried" changes the verb and adds "our" and moves "in Damascus" forward; EMET participial |
| 5 | "to Damascus I-was-journeying, to-bring also those there being, having-been-bound, to Jerusalem" | "I was traveling to Damascus to bring those who were there, bound to Jerusalem" | "was on my way there to drag those people back to Jerusalem in chains" | "and I was traveling to Damascus to bring those who were there, bound, to Jerusalem" | LOGOS clear; PNEUMA "drag...back...in chains" is vivid but interpretive expansion; EMET Greek word order |
| 5 | "that they-might-be-punished" | "that they might be punished" | "for punishment" | "that they might be punished" | EMET/LOGOS agree on purpose clause; PNEUMA reduces to prepositional phrase |
| 6 | "it-came-to-pass [to] me, journeying and drawing-near to Damascus" | "it happened that as I was traveling and drawing near to Damascus" | "as I was traveling -- it was about noon -- and approaching Damascus" | "it happened that as I was traveling and drawing near to Damascus" | LOGOS renders EGENETO naturally; PNEUMA interrupts with parenthetical time note (restructured from later in verse); EMET bracketed supplement |
| 6 | "about midday" | "about midday" | "about noon" | "about midday" | EMET/LOGOS agree on "midday" for PERI MESEMBRIAN; PNEUMA "noon" is acceptable synonym |
| 6 | "suddenly out-of the heaven a great light shone-around me" | "suddenly a great light from heaven shone around me" | "suddenly a blinding light from heaven blazed all around me" | "suddenly a great light from heaven shone around me" | LOGOS natural word order; PNEUMA "blinding" and "blazed" are interpretive intensifications (Greek PERIASTRAPTO = flash/shine around, HIKANON = great/sufficient, not "blinding"); EMET "out-of" is literal |
| 7 | "I-fell to the ground" | "I fell to the ground" | "I fell to the ground" | "I fell to the ground" | All three agree |
| 7 | "saying to-me, 'Saul, Saul, why are-you-persecuting me?'" | "saying to me, 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?'" | "saying to me, 'Saul, Saul, why are you hunting me down?'" | "saying to me, 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?'" | EMET/LOGOS "persecuting" for DIOKEIS; PNEUMA "hunting me down" is interpretive (carries predatory nuance not necessarily in Greek) |
| 8 | "Jesus the Nazorean" | "Jesus the Nazarene" | "Jesus the Nazarene" | "Jesus the Nazarene" | LOGOS/PNEUMA "Nazarene" for NAZORAIOS; EMET "Nazorean" preserves Greek form more precisely. SOPHIA follows majority "Nazarene" as more recognizable English |
| 8 | "whom you are-persecuting" | "whom you are persecuting" | "the one you are hunting down" | "whom you are persecuting" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "the one you are hunting down" restructures and repeats interpretive verb |
| 9 | "those being with me the light indeed beheld" | "those who were with me saw the light" | "The men with me saw the light" | "those who were with me saw the light" | LOGOS natural; EMET "indeed beheld" (MEN... ETHEASANTO) is literal but awkward word order; PNEUMA "the men" specifies gender not explicit in Greek (HOI... ONTES) |
| 9 | "but the voice they-did-not-hear of-the-[one] speaking to-me" | "but they did not hear the voice of the one speaking to me" | "but they could not make out the voice speaking to me" | "but they did not hear the voice of the one speaking to me" | LOGOS renders OUK EKOUSAN straightforwardly; PNEUMA "could not make out" is interpretive -- it resolves the Acts 9:7 tension by implying comprehension failure rather than auditory failure; EMET word order mirrors Greek. SOPHIA preserves "did not hear" without harmonizing |
| 10 | "'What shall-I-do, Lord?'" | "'What shall I do, Lord?'" | "'What should I do, Lord?'" | "'What shall I do, Lord?'" | EMET/LOGOS "shall" for deliberative subjunctive TI POIESO; PNEUMA "should" is acceptable but loses the formal register |
| 10 | "'Having-risen-up, go into Damascus'" | "'Rise and go into Damascus'" | "'Get up. Go into Damascus.'" | "'Rise and go into Damascus'" | LOGOS combines participle and imperative naturally; PNEUMA splits into two short sentences; EMET participial chain |
| 10 | "it-shall-be-told to-you concerning all-things which have-been-appointed for-you to-do" | "it will be told to you concerning all things which have been appointed for you to do" | "you will be told everything that has been laid out for you to do" | "it will be told to you concerning all things which have been appointed for you to do" | LOGOS natural rendering; PNEUMA "everything" for PANTON, "laid out" for TETAKTAI is interpretive; EMET hyphenated compounds |
| 11 | "as I-was-not-seeing from the glory of-that light" | "since I could not see because of the glory of that light" | "Since I could not see -- the brilliance of that light had blinded me" | "since I could not see because of the glory of that light" | LOGOS clear causal construction; PNEUMA adds parenthetical explanation "had blinded me" (not a separate clause in Greek); EMET "was-not-seeing" is awkward |
| 11 | "being-led-by-the-hand by those being-with me" | "being led by the hand by those who were with me" | "my companions took me by the hand and led me" | "being led by the hand by those who were with me" | EMET/LOGOS agree on passive participle; PNEUMA converts to active voice with "companions" (not in text) |
| 11 | "I-came into Damascus" | "I came into Damascus" | "led me into Damascus" | "I came into Damascus" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA merges with previous clause |
| 12 | "a certain Ananias, a devout man according-to the law" | "a certain Ananias, a devout man according to the law" | "A man named Ananias came to me. He was devout, faithful to the Law" | "a certain Ananias, a devout man according to the law" | EMET/LOGOS agree on structure; PNEUMA splits into two sentences, renders EUSEBES as both "devout" and "faithful to the Law" (double rendering), capitalizes "Law" |
| 12 | "being-witnessed-to by all the Jews dwelling-[there]" | "well spoken of by all the Jews dwelling there" | "well regarded by every Jew living there" | "well spoken of by all the Jews dwelling there" | LOGOS "well spoken of" for MARTUROUMENOS is idiomatic; PNEUMA "well regarded" slightly shifts meaning; EMET "being-witnessed-to" is literal but unclear in English |
| 13 | "having-come to me and having-stood-beside-[me]" | "came to me and standing beside me" | "He stood beside me" | "came to me and standing beside me" | LOGOS preserves both participles; PNEUMA drops "came to me" (ELTHON); EMET double participle |
| 13 | "'Brother Saul, receive-sight.'" | "'Brother Saul, receive your sight.'" | "'Brother Saul, receive your sight!'" | "'Brother Saul, receive your sight.'" | LOGOS/PNEUMA add possessive "your"; PNEUMA adds exclamation mark. SOPHIA follows LOGOS punctuation |
| 13 | "I in-that-same hour looked-up at him" | "in that very hour I looked up at him" | "at that very moment I looked up and could see him" | "in that very hour I looked up at him" | LOGOS natural word order; PNEUMA adds "and could see him" (interpretive expansion); EMET "that-same" for AUTE acceptable but "very" is more natural |
| 14 | "The God of-our fathers appointed you" | "The God of our fathers has appointed you" | "The God of our ancestors has handpicked you" | "The God of our fathers has appointed you" | LOGOS "has appointed" best for PROECHEIRISATO; PNEUMA "ancestors" for PATERON and "handpicked" is interpretive (adds connotation of personal selection beyond the Greek); EMET omits auxiliary "has" |
| 14 | "to-know his will" | "to know his will" | "to know his will" | "to know his will" | All three agree |
| 14 | "to-see the Righteous-One" | "to see the Righteous One" | "to see the Righteous One face to face" | "to see the Righteous One" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "face to face" (not in text). All three correctly capitalize as messianic title for TON DIKAION |
| 14 | "to-hear a voice from his mouth" | "to hear a voice from his mouth" | "to hear the words from his own mouth" | "to hear a voice from his mouth" | EMET/LOGOS "a voice" for PHONEN; PNEUMA "the words" is interpretive, adds "own" |
| 15 | "you-shall-be a witness for-him to all men" | "you will be a witness for him to all people" | "you will be his witness to all people" | "for you will be a witness for him to all people" | LOGOS "all people" for PANTAS ANTHROPOUS (gender-inclusive rendering); EMET "all men" is literal; PNEUMA "his witness" restructures genitive. SOPHIA adds "for" at beginning to capture HOTI causal conjunction |
| 15 | "of-what you-have-seen and heard" | "of what you have seen and heard" | "telling them what you have seen and heard" | "of what you have seen and heard" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "telling them" (not in text) |
| 16 | "And now, why are-you-delaying?" | "And now why do you delay?" | "So what are you waiting for?" | "And now why do you delay?" | LOGOS formal rendering of TI MELLEIS; PNEUMA "what are you waiting for?" is colloquial paraphrase; EMET "are-you-delaying" is acceptable |
| 16 | "Having-risen-up, be-baptized and wash-away your sins" | "Rise and be baptized and wash away your sins" | "Get up! Be baptized and have your sins washed away" | "Rise and be baptized and wash away your sins" | LOGOS combines naturally; PNEUMA "Get up!" with exclamation and passive "have your sins washed away" restructures middle voice; EMET participial |
| 16 | "having-called-upon his name" | "calling upon his name" | "calling on his name" | "calling upon his name" | EMET aorist participle "having-called-upon"; LOGOS/SOPHIA present participle "calling upon"; PNEUMA "on" vs "upon" minor preposition choice |
| 17 | "it-came-to-pass [to] me, having-returned to Jerusalem and [while] praying [of] me in the temple" | "it happened that when I had returned to Jerusalem and was praying in the temple" | "Later, after I had returned to Jerusalem and was praying in the temple" | "it happened that when I had returned to Jerusalem and was praying in the temple" | LOGOS renders EGENETO construction naturally; PNEUMA "Later" replaces EGENETO and adds temporal marker not in text; EMET multiple brackets |
| 17 | "I came-to-be in an ecstasy" | "I fell into a trance" | "I fell into a trance" | "I fell into a trance" | LOGOS/PNEUMA "trance" for EKSTASIS is natural English; EMET "ecstasy" preserves Greek etymology (EKSTASIS = standing-outside-oneself) but is misleading in modern English |
| 18 | "and to-see him saying to-me" | "and saw him saying to me" | "I saw him -- and he said to me" | "and I saw him saying to me" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA restructures with dash; EMET infinitive construction. SOPHIA adds "I" for clarity |
| 18 | "'Make-haste and go-out in quickness from Jerusalem'" | "'Make haste and go out quickly from Jerusalem'" | "'Hurry! Get out of Jerusalem at once'" | "'Make haste and go out quickly from Jerusalem'" | LOGOS formal; PNEUMA "Hurry! Get out...at once" is vivid but adds exclamation and restructures; EMET "in quickness" for EN TACHEI is literal |
| 18 | "they-will-not-receive your testimony concerning me" | "they will not receive your testimony concerning me" | "they will not accept your testimony about me" | "they will not receive your testimony concerning me" | EMET/LOGOS "receive" for PARADEXONTAI; PNEUMA "accept" acceptable synonym; "concerning" vs "about" is register difference |
| 19 | "'Lord, they themselves know that I was imprisoning and beating in every synagogue those believing upon you'" | "'Lord, they themselves know that I was imprisoning and beating in every synagogue those who believe in you'" | "'Lord, they know perfectly well that I went from one synagogue to the next, imprisoning and beating those who believed in you'" | "'Lord, they themselves know that I was imprisoning and beating in every synagogue those who believe in you'" | LOGOS preserves structure; PNEUMA "perfectly well" adds intensifier, "went from one synagogue to the next" restructures KATA TAS SUNAGOGAS, changes tense to "believed"; EMET "believing upon" is literal for PISTEUONTAS EPI SE |
| 20 | "when the blood of Stephen your witness was-being-poured-out" | "when the blood of Stephen your witness was being shed" | "when the blood of your witness Stephen was being poured out" | "when the blood of Stephen your witness was being shed" | LOGOS "shed" for EXECHUNNETO is idiomatic for blood; EMET "poured-out" is literal; PNEUMA reorders to "your witness Stephen" (different word order). All three correctly render MARTYS as "witness" |
| 20 | "I myself also was standing-by and consenting" | "I myself also was standing by and approving" | "I was standing right there -- giving my full approval" | "I myself also was standing by and approving" | LOGOS "approving" for SUNEUDOKON is precise; PNEUMA "right there" adds emphasis, "full approval" intensifies, dash restructures; EMET "consenting" is acceptable synonym |
| 20 | "guarding the garments of-those killing him" | "guarding the garments of those who were killing him" | "guarding the cloaks of those who killed him" | "guarding the garments of those who were killing him" | EMET/LOGOS "garments" for HIMATIA; PNEUMA "cloaks" is a specific garment type (acceptable but narrower). LOGOS imperfective "were killing" preserves ANAIROUNTON |
| 21 | "'Go, because I to nations far-off will-send-out you'" | "'Go, for I will send you far away to the Gentiles'" | "'Go. I am sending you far away -- to the Gentiles'" | "'Go, for I will send you far away to the Gentiles'" | LOGOS natural rendering; EMET "nations" for ETHNE is literal but "Gentiles" is the standard translation in this context; PNEUMA present tense "am sending" and dash for dramatic effect; EMET word order mirrors Greek |
| 22 | "they-were-hearing him until this word" | "They listened to him up to this word" | "The crowd listened to him up to that word" | "They listened to him up to this word" | LOGOS renders EKOUON naturally; PNEUMA adds "The crowd" as subject (not in text -- implied), changes "this" to "that"; EMET imperfective "were-hearing" |
| 22 | "they-raised their voice saying" | "they raised their voice saying" | "Then they erupted" | "and then they raised their voice saying" | EMET/LOGOS agree on EPERAN TEN PHONEN; PNEUMA "erupted" is dramatic paraphrase. SOPHIA adds "and then" for temporal flow |
| 22 | "'Take-away from the earth such-a-[one], for it-is-not-fitting [for] him to-live!'" | "'Away from the earth with such a one, for it is not fitting that he should live!'" | "'Wipe this man off the face of the earth! He does not deserve to live!'" | "'Away from the earth with such a one, for it is not fitting that he should live!'" | LOGOS preserves formal construction AIRE APO TES GES TON TOIOUTON; PNEUMA "Wipe...off the face of the earth" is vivid paraphrase, "does not deserve to live" restructures OU KATHEKON; EMET brackets |
| 23 | "they-were-crying-out and tearing the garments and throwing dust into the air" | "they were crying out and throwing off their garments and casting dust into the air" | "They were screaming, ripping their cloaks off, and hurling dust into the air" | "they were crying out and throwing off their garments and casting dust into the air" | LOGOS renders three imperfectives naturally; PNEUMA "screaming" (stronger than KRAUGAZONTON), "ripping" (more violent than RHIPTOUNTON), "hurling" (more forceful than BALLONTON), "cloaks" for HIMATIA; EMET "tearing" for RHIPTOUNTON (which means "throwing off," not "tearing") is a mistranslation |
| 24 | "the commander ordered him to-be-brought into the barracks" | "the commander ordered him to be brought into the barracks" | "the commanding officer ordered Paul brought into the barracks" | "the commander ordered him to be brought into the barracks" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "commanding officer" for CHILIARCHOS (longer title), names "Paul" explicitly, drops "to be" |
| 24 | "saying [for him] to-be-examined by scourging" | "saying that he should be examined by flogging" | "He directed that he be examined under flogging" | "directing that he should be examined by flogging" | SOPHIA blends: "directing" from PNEUMA's restructuring with LOGOS's "flogging" for MASTIXIN (more precise than EMET's "scourging" though both valid); PNEUMA "under flogging" vs "by flogging" |
| 24 | "that he-might-know for what cause they-were-so-crying-out against him" | "so that he might find out for what reason they were crying out against him in this way" | "to find out why the crowd was raging against him like this" | "so that he might find out for what reason they were crying out against him in this way" | LOGOS "find out" for EPIGNO; PNEUMA "crowd was raging" is interpretive expansion; EMET "for what cause" is literal |
| 25 | "as they-stretched-him-out with the straps" | "as they stretched him out with the straps" | "as they stretched him out for the lash" | "as they stretched him out with the straps" | EMET/LOGOS agree on TOIS HIMASIN "with the straps"; PNEUMA "for the lash" changes instrumental dative to purposive |
| 25 | "'Is-it-lawful for-you a man [who is] a Roman and uncondemned to-scourge?'" | "'Is it lawful for you to flog a man who is a Roman and uncondemned?'" | "'Is it legal for you to flog a Roman citizen who hasn't even been tried?'" | "'Is it lawful for you to flog a man who is a Roman and uncondemned?'" | LOGOS word order natural; PNEUMA "legal" for EXESTIN (acceptable), "citizen" adds word not in text (ROMAION = Roman), "hasn't even been tried" paraphrases AKATAKRITON; EMET word order mirrors Greek |
| 26 | "having-heard, the centurion, having-gone-to the commander, reported" | "when the centurion heard this, he went to the commander and reported" | "When the centurion heard this, he went straight to the commanding officer and reported it" | "when the centurion heard this, he went to the commander and reported" | LOGOS temporal clause natural; PNEUMA "straight to" adds emphasis, "commanding officer" for CHILIARCHOS, adds "it" as object; EMET double participle |
| 26 | "'What are-you-about to-do? For this man is a Roman'" | "'What are you about to do? For this man is a Roman'" | "'Watch what you're doing,' he said. 'This man is a Roman citizen'" | "'What are you about to do? For this man is a Roman'" | EMET/LOGOS agree on TI MELLEIS POIEIN; PNEUMA "Watch what you're doing" is colloquial paraphrase, adds "citizen" (not in text -- ROMAIOS = Roman) |
| 27 | "having-come-to-[him] the commander said to-him" | "the commander came and said to him" | "The commanding officer came over and asked Paul directly" | "the commander came and said to him" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "came over" adds directional detail, "asked...directly" changes EIPEN (said) to interpretive, names "Paul"; EMET participle |
| 27 | "'Tell me, are you a Roman?' And he said, 'Yes.'" | "'Tell me, are you a Roman?' And he said, 'Yes.'" | "'Tell me -- are you a Roman citizen?' 'Yes,' he said." | "'Tell me, are you a Roman?' And he said, 'Yes.'" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds dash, inserts "citizen" (not in Greek), reorders dialogue tag |
| 28 | "'I [for] a great sum acquired this citizenship'" | "'I acquired this citizenship with a great sum'" | "'I paid an enormous sum for my citizenship'" | "'I acquired this citizenship with a great sum'" | LOGOS natural word order for POLLOU KEPHALAIOU TEN POLITEIAN TAUTEN EKTESAMEN; PNEUMA "paid an enormous sum" is interpretive ("enormous" stronger than POLLOU = "great"); EMET brackets |
| 28 | "'But I have-even-been-born-[one]'" | "'But I was even born a citizen'" | "'But I was born one'" | "'But I was even born a citizen'" | LOGOS supplies "a citizen" naturally for elliptical EGO DE KAI GEGENNEMAI; PNEUMA drops KAI ("even") which adds rhetorical force; EMET brackets, hyphenated compound |
| 29 | "Immediately therefore those being-about to-examine him stood-away from him" | "Therefore those who were about to examine him immediately withdrew from him" | "Immediately those who were about to interrogate him backed away" | "Therefore those who were about to examine him immediately withdrew from him" | LOGOS places "therefore" (OUN) and "immediately" (EUTHEOS) well; PNEUMA "interrogate" for ANETAZEIN (which means examine/investigate, not necessarily interrogate), "backed away" informal; EMET "stood-away" is literal for APESTE |
| 29 | "the commander also was-afraid, having-learned that he-is a Roman and that he-had bound him" | "the commander also was afraid, having learned that he was a Roman and that he had bound him" | "the commanding officer himself was alarmed when he realized he had put a Roman citizen in chains" | "the commander also was afraid, having learned that he was a Roman and that he had bound him" | LOGOS preserves participial structure; PNEUMA "alarmed" for EPHOBTHE (was afraid -- "alarmed" is weaker), "realized" for EPIGNOUS (learned), "put a Roman citizen in chains" restructures and adds "citizen" and "in chains" (text says "bound" DEDEKEN); EMET present tense "he-is" preserves Greek but English requires past |
| 30 | "on-the next-day, wanting to-know the certainty" | "on the next day, desiring to know the certainty" | "The next day, determined to get to the bottom of" | "on the next day, desiring to know the certainty" | LOGOS "desiring" for BOULOMENOS; PNEUMA "determined to get to the bottom of" is colloquial paraphrase; EMET "wanting" is acceptable |
| 30 | "the-[reason] why he-is-being-accused by the Jews" | "of why he was being accused by the Jews" | "what the Jews were actually charging Paul with" | "of why he was being accused by the Jews" | LOGOS natural English; PNEUMA "actually" adds emphasis, "charging...with" restructures, names "Paul"; EMET brackets |
| 30 | "he-released him" | "he released him" | "he released him from his bonds" | "he released him" | EMET/LOGOS agree on ELUSEN AUTON; PNEUMA adds "from his bonds" (interpretive expansion -- some later manuscripts add APO TON DESMON but Sinaiticus reads simply ELUSEN AUTON) |
| 30 | "commanded the chief-priests and all the council to-come-together" | "commanded the chief priests and all the council to assemble" | "ordered the chief priests and the entire council to assemble" | "commanded the chief priests and all the council to assemble" | LOGOS "assemble" for SUNELTHEIN; PNEUMA "ordered" for EKELEUSEN (both "commanded" and "ordered" valid), "entire" for PAN/HOLON; EMET "come-together" is literal |
| 30 | "having-brought-down Paul he-set-[him] before them" | "he brought Paul down and set him before them" | "brought Paul down and stood him before them" | "he brought Paul down and set him before them" | LOGOS "set" for ESTESEN; PNEUMA "stood" is literal (HISTEMI = to stand/set) and acceptable; EMET participle |

---

## Key Theological Disagreements

### 1. Verse 2 -- "Hebrew Language" (Hebrew or Aramaic?)
All three translators render TE HEBRAIDI DIALEKTO as "Hebrew language/dialect." The referent is almost certainly Aramaic, the spoken Semitic tongue of first-century Palestine, but the text says "Hebrew." SOPHIA preserves the text as written, consistent with the decision at 21:40. EMET uses "dialect" while LOGOS/PNEUMA use "language" -- SOPHIA follows "language" as more natural in English, since DIALEKTOS in Koine carries the broader sense of "language."

### 2. Verse 9 -- The Companions' Perception (Acts 9:7 vs. 22:9)
This is one of the most discussed apparent contradictions in Acts. In 9:7, the companions AKOUONTES TES PHONES (genitive -- "hearing the voice/sound"). In 22:9, they TEN PHONEN OUK EKOUSAN (accusative -- "did not hear the voice"). The grammatical case difference may indicate hearing a sound (genitive) versus hearing with comprehension (accusative).
- **EMET:** "the voice they-did-not-hear of-the-one speaking to-me" -- preserves denial literally with Greek word order
- **LOGOS:** "they did not hear the voice of the one speaking to me" -- clear English rendering of the denial
- **PNEUMA:** "they could not make out the voice speaking to me" -- interprets the tension by rendering "did not hear" as "could not make out," implying partial perception
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. The text says they did not hear; PNEUMA's "could not make out" is an interpretive harmonization. Each passage stands as written.

### 3. Verse 14 -- "The Righteous One" (TON DIKAION)
All three translators unanimously render this as "the Righteous One" with capitalization, recognizing it as a messianic/christological title (cf. Acts 3:14, 7:52, James 5:6). No disagreement on this point.
- **PNEUMA** adds "face to face" and "handpicked" -- both interpretive expansions. SOPHIA retains "appointed" (LOGOS) and omits "face to face."

### 4. Verse 20 -- MARTYS for Stephen: "Witness" vs. "Martyr"
All three translators render MARTUROS as "witness," which is correct for the semantic range in the first century. The word had not yet narrowed to mean exclusively one who dies for the faith. However, Stephen's death while testifying provides the very context in which the semantic shift from "witness" to "martyr" began.
- **EMET:** "witness" with note on later development
- **LOGOS:** "witness" with note on the weight the word carries
- **PNEUMA:** "witness" with note on the blood-language carrying martyrological weight
- **SOPHIA:** "witness" -- unanimous. The blood imagery ("being shed") makes the martyrological dimension clear without anachronistic vocabulary.

### 5. Verse 20 -- "Shed" vs. "Poured Out" (EXECHUNNETO)
- **EMET:** "was-being-poured-out" (literal for EKCHEO)
- **LOGOS:** "was being shed" (idiomatic English for blood)
- **PNEUMA:** "was being poured out"
- **SOPHIA:** "was being shed" -- the English collocation "blood...shed" is the natural rendering; "poured out" is literal but less idiomatic for blood in English.

### 6. Verse 21 -- "Nations" vs. "Gentiles" (ETHNE)
- **EMET:** "nations" (literal ETHNE)
- **LOGOS/PNEUMA:** "Gentiles"
- **SOPHIA:** "Gentiles" -- in the context of Jewish/non-Jewish distinction, ETHNE carries the technical sense "Gentiles" rather than the geographic "nations." This word is the trigger for the mob's eruption (v. 22).

### 7. Verse 25 -- The Citizenship Question (Legal Formulation)
- **EMET:** "Is-it-lawful for-you a man [who is] a Roman and uncondemned to-scourge?"
- **LOGOS:** "Is it lawful for you to flog a man who is a Roman and uncondemned?"
- **PNEUMA:** "Is it legal for you to flog a Roman citizen who hasn't even been tried?"
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "citizen" (not in Greek ROMAION) and paraphrases AKATAKRITON ("uncondemned") as "hasn't even been tried." The Greek preserves the dual legal protection -- Roman status AND lack of trial -- in two adjectives. LOGOS's "uncondemned" retains the formal legal register.

### 8. Verse 28 -- Citizenship by Purchase vs. Birth (POLITEIA)
The commander's POLLOU KEPHALAIOU ("with a great sum") reveals purchased citizenship, likely under Claudius. Paul's EGO DE KAI GEGENNEMAI ("But I was even born [one]") asserts inherited citizenship of higher social standing.
- **EMET:** "I have-even-been-born-[one]" -- preserves KAI ("even") but bracketed supplement
- **LOGOS:** "But I was even born a citizen" -- supplies "a citizen" for the ellipsis, retains "even"
- **PNEUMA:** "But I was born one" -- drops KAI ("even"), which carries significant rhetorical force
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. The "even" (KAI) is important: it intensifies the contrast. Supplying "a citizen" for the ellipsis is necessary for English clarity.

### 9. Verse 23 -- "Tearing" vs. "Throwing Off" Garments (RHIPTOUNTON)
- **EMET:** "tearing the garments" -- this misrenders RHIPTO, which means to throw/cast/fling, not to tear
- **LOGOS:** "throwing off their garments" -- correct rendering of RHIPTOUNTON TA HIMATIA
- **PNEUMA:** "ripping their cloaks off" -- "ripping" carries the tearing sense; "cloaks" for HIMATIA
- **SOPHIA:** Follows LOGOS. The action is throwing off garments in fury, not tearing them (which would be SCHIZO or DIARREGNUMI).

### 10. Verse 17 -- "Ecstasy" vs. "Trance" (EKSTASIS)
- **EMET:** "ecstasy" -- preserves Greek etymology but misleading in modern English (connotes euphoria)
- **LOGOS/PNEUMA:** "trance" -- the correct modern English equivalent for the visionary state
- **SOPHIA:** "trance" -- EKSTASIS in this context denotes a visionary state during prayer, not ecstatic joy. "Trance" avoids the modern misreading.

---

*End of Disagreement Log -- Acts Chapter 22*
